text: The laparoscopic view reveals significant fibrosis and hypertrophic changes in the left lobe of the liver.
The surgeon prepares to set up a Pringle maneuver, a technique used to control blood flow to the liver during surgery, although it is not utilized in this case.
The surgeon clips and staples the right polar vein, highlighting the section with precise vascular control techniques.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to visualize the mid-hepatic vein and identify the locations of hepatic veins V8 and V5 within the liver.
The surgeon performs a hanging maneuver during the right hepatectomy procedure in a fibrotic liver.
The surgeon performs a superficial transection using a harmonic device, visibly cutting through the tissue layers.
The surgeon performs deeper transection using a combination of CUSA and clipping techniques to navigate around vascular structures, ensuring precise separation and preservation of these vessels.
The surgeon navigates around the portal vein branches, which contain embolic material from a prior portal vein embolization, visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a stapler to secure the right polar pedicle, followed by deep transection with a combination of Chiusa and harmonic devices, clearing the path for stapling V-eight and then the right departed vein.
The surgeon uses the CUSA device to perform a clean dissection around the vessels, preparing them for safe stapling.
The surgeon approaches the hilum, isolating the right pedicle and ligating the right portal vein, with visible manipulation of the vascular structures.
The surgeon performs a partial parenchymal transection, focusing on the crossing portal pedicles to promote hypertrophy of the blood flow.
Fifteen days post-procedure, the video shows hypertrophic changes in the left lobe of the liver, with visible inflammatory adhesions complicating the re-approach to the transection plane.
The surgeon retrieves the previously isolated right hepatic artery and dissects back towards the hilum, navigating through the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates through adhesions from the prior surgery to reach the right hepatic pedicle and prepares to staple it.
The surgeon continues with the deeper parenchymal transection, progressing towards completing the hepatectomy.
The surgeon uses the CUSA (Cavitron Ultrasonic Surgical Aspirator) to dissect intraparenchymal structures, highlighting its critical role in the procedure.
The video shows a nodular liver with dense scarring and visible ascites in the abdominal cavity.
During the parenchymal transection, dense scar tissue is visible surrounding the vascular structures, requiring careful dissection.
The surgeon uses a combination of harmonic, bipolar energy, and primarily CUSA to clear intraparenchymal structures, carefully minimizing the tissue amount for the staple during transection.
After completing the transection, the surgeon treats the cut surface with bipolar energy and sutures, applying a technique known as 'bite lick'.
The surgeon exposes the superficial but posterior lesion by taking down the triangular ligament, enhancing visibility for further surgical intervention.
Under ultrasound guidance, the surgeon identifies a transection plane with a portal pedicle crossing it, providing visual confirmation of the anatomical structures involved in the hepatectomy.
The surgeon performs parenchymal transection using a combination of vessel sealer and bipolar forceps, which also aids in the fracture of the liver parenchyma.

The robotic instruments, with enhanced dexterity and strength, are used to transect the cirrhotic parenchyma. The dissection around the portal pedicle is challenging, leading to the use of sutures to secure the pedicle for safe control.
The surgeon completes the transection of the portal pedicle using a vessel sealer and bipolar forceps, resulting in a significant sparing of the surrounding parenchyma.
The surgeon navigates significant adhesions from a prior cholecystectomy, working within a tight space to delineate the transection area above the portal pedicle.

During the transection, the surgeon uses a vessel sealer on the portal pedicle, encountering bleeding that is initially managed with simple compression. A short Pringle maneuver is then applied for under six minutes to achieve hemostasis and complete the transection.
